function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing retrospectively recasts historical financials under common-control accounting following the mergers. What historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment?

steps.reasoning_content:
The question asks about the differences between common-control accounting and purchase accounting, specifically what historical information becomes "no longer observable" when financials are retrospectively recast under common-control accounting.

Let me first retrieve the relevant disclosures from the SpaceX S-1 filing about the common-control accounting treatment and the mergers with xAI and X Holdings.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Good, the filing has been parsed. Let me now retrieve information about the common-control accounting treatment, the mergers, and any discussion of what historical information is no longer observable.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what disclosures are made about common-control accounting treatment, the mergers with xAI and X Holdings, and what historical information is no longer observable or no longer reflects standalone SpaceX operations? Include any discussion of purchase accounting vs common-control accounting.

results.content:
Based on the prospectus disclosures, here is a summary of the accounting treatment for the mergers with xAI and X Holdings, and what that means for the historical financial information:

### **1. Accounting Treatment: Common Control vs. Purchase Accounting**

The mergers were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**. This is different from a typical business combination accounted for under purchase accounting (ASC 805).

*   **Why Common Control?** Elon Musk was deemed to have a **controlling financial interest** in SpaceX, xAI, and X through his majority voting interest in each entity during the periods presented.
*   **Key Principle of Common-Control Accounting:** The assets and liabilities of the combining entities are carried forward at their **historical (book) values**. This means:
    *   **No New Goodwill or Intangible Assets:** The consolidated financial statements do not recognize any new goodwill or other intangible assets that would typically arise from a purchase price allocation in a standard acquisition.
    *   **Retrospective Combination:** The financial statements are **retroactively adjusted for all periods presented** to include the historical results, assets, and liabilities of xAI and X as if they had always been part of the consolidated group.
    *   **Elimination of Intercompany Transactions:** All historical related-party transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X have been eliminated in consolidation for all periods.

In contrast, **purchase accounting** would involve valuing the acquired assets and liabilities at fair value on the acquisition date, often resulting in new goodwill and intangible assets on the balance sheet, and would only combine financial results from the acquisition date forward.

### **2. Disclosures About the Mergers**

The prospectus makes extensive disclosures:
*   **Timeline:** SpaceX acquired xAI on **February 2, 2026**. Prior to that, on **March 28, 2025**, xAI had acquired X (including X.AI Corp.). Twitter was acquired by Elon Musk in October 2022.
*   **Transaction Structure:** Each merger was effected through a share exchange with specific conversion ratios (e.g., X Common Stock converted to 2.776 shares of xAI Common Stock).
*   **Change in Reporting Entity:** The retrospective combination is explicitly described as a **"change in reporting entity"** under accounting standards.
*   **Omission of Separate Financials:** Because the consolidated financials already reflect the combined entity retroactively, **separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided**.

### **3. What Historical Information is No Longer Observable / Standalone?**

As a result of the common-control accounting, **no financial data reflects the standalone operations of SpaceX prior to the mergers.** All historical financial information presented in the prospectus (including revenue, expenses, assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity for periods before 2025 and 2026) is the **consolidated result of SpaceX, xAI, and X combined.**

*   **Impact on Comparability:** Investors cannot analyze SpaceX's standalone financial performance, margins, or capital structure before it absorbed xAI and X. The historical data is a blend of all three companies.
*   **Capital Structure Presentation:** The capital stock and shareholders' equity sections reflect the combined historical equity of all entities, adjusted for the merger exchange ratios and a subsequent 2026 stock split.

### **4. Other Key Accounting Notes**

*   **Stock Split:** A five-for-one forward stock split in May 2026 is reflected retroactively in all share and per-share data.
*   **Redeemable Preferred Stock:** xAI's redeemable convertible preferred stock was converted to SpaceX Common Stock upon the xAI Merger, but its historical balance is presented as preferred stock until the conversion date (not recast retroactively as common stock).

In summary, the common-control accounting treatment means **the prospectus presents a historical financial view of the combined SpaceX/xAI/X entity from inception**, with assets at book value and no new acquisition-related accounting entries. This provides a cohesive historical record of the consolidated group but **obscures the separate financial history and standalone performance of SpaceX as it existed before these transformative mergers.**

steps.reasoning_content: The retrieval has provided a good overview. Let me also search for more specific language about what is "no longer observable" and any specific comparison to purchase accounting treatment.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find any specific statements about what historical information is no longer observable, no longer available, or cannot be separately determined as a result of common-control accounting. Also look for any discussion about fair value of acquired assets, goodwill, intangible assets that would have been recognized under purchase accounting but are not under common-control accounting. Include any risk factors or limitations mentioned about the retrospective recast.

results.content:
Based on the provided financial statement excerpts, here are the key points regarding what historical information is no longer observable or available due to common-control accounting, along with related risk factors and limitations:

### **Direct Impacts of Common-Control Accounting:**
1. **No Separate Historical Financials:**  
   - "Separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided" because the consolidated financial statements already reflect their reorganization under common control.

2. **Elimination of Intercompany Transactions:**  
   - "All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation." This means individual transaction details between SpaceX, xAI, and X are no longer observable in the reported historical data.

3. **No Recognition of New Goodwill or Intangible Assets:**  
   - "No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded." Under purchase accounting, these would typically arise, but common-control rules require carrying over historical values without such adjustments.

4. **Historical Equity Adjustments Obscured:**  
   - Capital stock and shareholders’ equity are presented as a continuation of SpaceX’s historical equity, combined with the historical equity of X and xAI as adjusted by exchange ratios. This blended presentation obscures the individual equity structures prior to the mergers.

5. **Redeemable Preferred Stock Conversion:**  
   - xAI’s historical redeemable convertible preferred stock is presented as SpaceX common stock only from the xAI Merger date onward. Its historical classification and terms are no longer separately visible in prior periods.

6. **Retroactive Stock Split Adjustment:**  
   - The 2026 five-for-one stock split is applied retroactively to all periods, making historical share and per-share data unobservable without adjustment.

### **Risk Factors and Limitations:**
1. **Forward-Looking Caution:**  
   - Historical data is "not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period," and interim results are "not necessarily indicative of the results to be expected for the full fiscal period."

2. **Supply Chain Concentration Risks:**  
   - Dependence on "limited number of suppliers" and "sole or limited-source suppliers" for critical inputs in Space, Connectivity, and AI segments is highlighted as a risk that could adversely affect operations.

3. **Goodwill Impairment Risk:**  
   - While no impairments were identified for 2023–2025, the annual testing process indicates ongoing exposure to potential future impairments if fair values decline.

4. **Spectrum Acquisition Pending Recognition:**  
   - The $17 billion Spectrum Transactions are contingent on regulatory approvals and closing conditions, meaning related intangible assets are not yet recognized, creating future reporting uncertainty.

5. **Use of Estimates and Judgments:**  
   - Financial statements require "significant judgment and use of estimates" in areas like revenue recognition, tax liabilities, inventory reserves, and fair value measurements. Actual results could differ from these estimates.

6. **Change in Reporting Entity:**  
   - The retrospective combination of X and xAI constitutes a "change in reporting entity," which limits comparability to prior periods when SpaceX operated independently.

These points collectively indicate that while common-control accounting provides a blended historical view, it masks the separate operational and financial histories of the merged entities and introduces dependencies on assumptions and future conditions.

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me also look for any specific discussion comparing common-control accounting to purchase accounting, and particularly what financial metrics or valuations would have been different.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, find the specific accounting policy notes about the reorganization of entities under common control, the mergers with xAI and X Holdings, and any discussion of fair value, step-up in basis, or purchase price allocation. Also find any discussion about what would be different under purchase accounting (ASC 805) and what standalone historical information for SpaceX is no longer separately determinable or observable.

results.content:
# Common-Control Reorganization Accounting for the xAI and X Mergers

Below is every relevant passage from the provided text, organized by the specific topics you asked about.

---

## 1. The Accounting Framework: Common-Control Reorganization

The foundational policy statement appears in the preamble and is repeated throughout:

> **"The Mergers have been accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control as Mr. Elon Musk had a controlling financial interest in the Company, xAI and X through his majority voting interest in each such entity during the years presented in these consolidated financial statements."**

This is the single most important sentence. Because Elon Musk held majority voting interest in **all three entities simultaneously**, the accounting treatment is not a purchase/business combination under ASC 805 — it is a reorganization under common control. The "acquiring" entity (SpaceX) merely absorbs entities that were already effectively under the same ultimate control.

---

## 2. Retrospective Combination at Historical Carrying Amounts (No Fair Value, No Step-Up, No Goodwill)

The text states explicitly and repeatedly:

> **"The Company's consolidated financial statements have been prepared to reflect the retrospective combination of the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts for all periods presented."**

And:

> **"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded."**

This is the critical distinction. Under a common-control reorganization:

- **No purchase price allocation (PPA)** is performed.
- **No step-up in basis** of acquired assets to fair value.
- **No goodwill** is recognized.
- **No new intangible assets** are recorded (e.g., no customer relationships, no technology intangibles, no brand value from the X/Twitter acquisition or the xAI acquisition).

All assets and liabilities of X Holdings (Twitter/X), X.AI Corp., and xAI are carried at their **pre-existing historical book values** as if they had always been part of SpaceX's consolidated group.

---

## 3. Elimination of Intercompany Transactions

The text states:

> **"All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation."**

This is reinforced in the accounting policies note (Note 2):

> **"The consolidated financial statements include the accounts of the Company and its wholly owned subsidiaries. All intercompany balances and transactions have been eliminated in consolidation."**

Any transactions that occurred between SpaceX, xAI, X Holdings, and X.AI Corp. during the historical periods — for example, SpaceX providing launch services to xAI, or xAI providing AI compute to X — are **removed** in the consolidated presentation. This prevents double-counting of revenue and expenses among entities that are now combined.

---

## 4. Capital Stock and Equity Adjustments

The text explains how equity is presented:

> **"The capital stock and shareholders' equity for all periods presented reflects a continuation of the historical SpaceX capital stock and shareholders' equity, combined with the historical capital stock and shareholders' equity of X and xAI merged under common control, as adjusted by the respective exchange ratios used to effect the Mergers."**

The exchange ratios were:

- **X Merger (March 28, 2025):** Each class of X Holdings Corp. common stock converted into **2.776 shares** of xAI Common Stock of the same class. Each class of X.AI Corp. common stock converted into **1.000 share** of xAI Common Stock of the same class.
- **xAI Merger (February 2, 2026):** Each share of xAI Common Stock converted into **0.1433 shares** of SpaceX Common Stock (on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis).

These ratios are applied **retroactively** to all historical share counts. The combined exchange ratio for X Holdings common stock into SpaceX common stock would therefore be 2.776 × 0.1433 = **0.3978 SpaceX shares per X Holdings share**.

The text also notes:

> **"No fractional SpaceX shares were issued and all share amounts were rounded up to the nearest whole number."**

And:

> **"Any shares of xAI Common Stock previously held by the Company were canceled and retired and did not receive any consideration."** (Elimination of treasury-equivalent cross-holdings.)

---

## 5. Treatment of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock — The Exception

One important nuance is how xAI's preferred stock was handled differently:

> **"The capital stock and shareholders' equity for all periods presented reflects a continuation of the historical SpaceX capital stock and shareholders' equity, combined with the historical capital stock and shareholders' equity of X and xAI merged under common control, as adjusted by the respective exchange ratios used to effect the Mergers, **except for xAI's historical redeemable convertible preferred stock.**"**

And in more detail:

> **"Although xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock converted into SpaceX Common Stock upon the xAI Merger closing, the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock balances are presented as Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock in the consolidated financial statements for all periods presented."**

> **"Because the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was legally outstanding during all historical periods prior to the xAI Merger and represented a separate equity class of a legally distinct predecessor entity, the conversion of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock into SpaceX Common Stock is recognized only in the period in which the exchange actually occurs, and not retrospectively."**

> **"Accordingly, the historical consolidated balance sheets and consolidated statements of redeemable convertible preferred stock and shareholders' equity reflect the xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock as outstanding xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock consistent with its legal form and rights during those periods and are not recast on an as-converted basis."**

> **"The impact of the conversion will be presented prospectively in the period of the merger (Q1 2026)."**

So while xAI **common stock** is retroactively shown as-if-converted into SpaceX common stock for all periods, xAI's **redeemable convertible preferred stock** remains on the balance sheet as its own class through the merger date, and only converts to SpaceX common stock in Q1 2026.

The preferred stock tables show this: as of December 31, 2025, xAI's Series A through Series E Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock are listed separately with their own dividend rates, initial prices, authorized shares, outstanding shares, liquidation preferences, and net carrying values (totaling ~$31.7 billion net carrying value across ~1.9 billion shares outstanding).

The conversion terms for the preferred stock upon the xAI Merger closing were:

> **"each share of xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock (other than xAI Series A Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock) was converted into 75.46 shares of SpaceX Class B Common Stock per preferred share (on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis)."**

For Series A specifically, eligible service providers could elect cash of $75.46 per share instead.

---

## 6. The Warrant Treatment

> **"xAI also issued warrants to customer that were outstanding as of the effective date of the xAI Merger, which had a ten-year term originally set to expire in 2035, with an exercise price equal to the par value of the stock, and vesting terms that resulted in the warrants vesting proportionally to the payments received under the related agreement. The closing of the xAI Merger triggered an acceleration clause in which all outstanding xAI warrants, both vested and unvested components, were automatically exercised on a cashless basis and converted into fully vested SpaceX Class A Common Stock at the exchange ratio of 0.1433 (on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis)."**

---

## 7. Change in Reporting Entity

The text explicitly identifies this as a change in reporting entity (rather than a business combination):

> **"This presentation constitutes a change in reporting entity."**

Under ASC 805 (Business Combinations), an acquisition of a business from an unrelated party requires purchase accounting — recognizing assets at fair value, recording goodwill, etc. Under common-control transactions, the guidance is different (primarily in ASC 805-50 and related guidance): the receiving entity combines the net assets at historical cost and recasts prior periods as if the entities had always been combined.

---

## 8. What Would Be Different Under Purchase Accounting (ASC 805)

The text **does not explicitly discuss** ASC 805 or what would differ under purchase accounting. However, the following statements are the **inverse** of what purchase accounting would require — each negation implicitly tells you what purchase accounting would do:

| What the text says (common control) | What purchase accounting (ASC 805) would require |
|---|---|
| "No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded" | Goodwill and identifiable intangible assets (customer relationships, technology, brand/trademarks) would be recognized at fair value |
| "Retrospective combination of the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts" | Assets and liabilities would be measured at acquisition-date fair value (step-up in basis) |
| "All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation" | This would also occur under purchase accounting, but the period in which the elimination begins would be the acquisition date, not retrospectively |
| "Separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided" | The pre-acquisition historical financials of the acquiree would typically be disclosed separately in business combination disclosures |
| The transaction is characterized as a "change in reporting entity" | It would be characterized as a business combination with required disclosures of purchase price allocation, contingent consideration, acquisition-related costs, etc. |

The practical significance is enormous. Given that:
- X Holdings (Twitter) was acquired by Musk in October 2022 (likely at a significant premium to any prior book value, after which Musk made substantial investments)
- xAI was founded in 2023 and raised billions in capital (Series A through E preferred stock with net carrying values totaling ~$31.7 billion)
- The combined liquidation preference of all xAI preferred stock alone is ~$31.7 billion

Under purchase accounting, SpaceX would need to:
1. Determine the fair value of consideration transferred
2. Fair-value all assets and liabilities of xAI and X Holdings (including the X/Twitter platform, user base, Grok AI models, infrastructure, etc.)
3. Record any difference as goodwill
4. Amortize finite-lived intangible assets prospectively

None of this occurs under common-control accounting. The historical book values carry through unchanged.

---

## 9. Standalone Historical Information That Is No Longer Separately Determinable

The text states:

> **"As the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization of entities under common control for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided."**

This is the key statement. Because all periods have been **retrospectively recast** to present SpaceX, xAI, X Holdings, and X.AI Corp. as a single combined entity:

- **SpaceX's standalone historical financial statements** (prior to absorbing xAI and X) are **no longer separately presented** or determinable from the consolidated statements
- **xAI's standalone historical financial statements** are not separately provided
- **X Holdings' (Twitter/X) standalone historical financial statements** are not separately provided
- **X.AI Corp.'s standalone historical financial statements** are not separately provided

The financial statements show **only the combined, consolidated view** for all periods. A reader cannot extract, for example, what SpaceX's revenue or net income was in 2024 **without** X/Twitter and xAI included, because those figures have been combined retroactively.

Additionally, the elimination of intercompany transactions means that the standalone economics of inter-entity relationships (e.g., SpaceX launching Starlink satellites that serve X's infrastructure, or xAI providing Grok capabilities to the X platform) are invisible in the consolidated presentation.

---

## Summary

The accounting treatment is a **common-control reorganization** — not a purchase/business combination — because Elon Musk controlled all entities throughout. The key consequences are:

1. **No fair value step-up** of any assets or liabilities
2. **No goodwill or new intangible assets** recognized
3. **Historical carrying amounts** are preserved
4. **Retrospective recasting** of all periods (as if the entities had always been combined)
5. **Intercompany transactions eliminated** for all periods
6. **Exchange ratios** (2.776x for X → xAI; 0.1433x for xAI → SpaceX) applied retroactively to share counts, with a 5-for-1 stock split also retroactively applied
7. **xAI preferred stock** treated as its own class until conversion at merger close (not retrospectively recast)
8. **No standalone historical statements** are provided for any of the individual predecessor entities
9. The entire presentation is classified as a **change in reporting entity**, not a business combination

steps.reasoning_content:
This is very comprehensive. Let me now compose a thorough answer to the question about what historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment.

The key distinction is between common-control accounting (what SpaceX uses) and purchase accounting (ASC 805). The question asks what historical information is "no longer observable" as a result of the common-control approach relative to what would have been observable under purchase accounting.

Under common-control accounting:
1. Assets and liabilities are carried at historical book values (no fair value step-up)
2. All periods are retrospectively recast as if the entities had always been combined
3. No goodwill or new intangible assets are recorded
4. Separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided


5. Intercompany transactions are eliminated for all periods

So the critical distinction is that under common-control accounting, I lose visibility into the individual fair values of assets and liabilities at acquisition, the standalone financial performance of each entity before consolidation, and any purchase price allocation that would have revealed how much of the acquisition price went to goodwill versus identifiable assets. This is quite different from what purchase accounting would have shown me—it would have given me a clear snapshot of the acquisition-date fair values and a breakdown of goodwill separately from other intangible assets.

arguments.final_result:
## Historical Information No Longer Observable Under Common-Control Accounting Relative to Purchase Accounting

The SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026 explicitly states that the mergers with xAI (completed February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (completed via xAI on March 28, 2025) "have been accounted for as reorganizations of entities under common control as Mr. Elon Musk had a controlling financial interest in the Company, xAI and X through his majority voting interest in each such entity during the years presented." This treatment — a "change in reporting entity" rather than a business combination under ASC 805 — renders several categories of historical information **no longer observable**:

---

### 1. Standalone Historical Financial Performance of Each Predecessor Entity

The filing states: *"As the consolidated financial statements already reflect the reorganization of entities under common control for all periods presented, separate financial statements of xAI and X are not provided."*

Under **common-control accounting**, all historical periods are **retrospectively recast** to present SpaceX, xAI, X Holdings, and X.AI Corp. as a single combined entity from inception. This means:

- **SpaceX's standalone revenue, margins, net income, and balance sheet** for any period prior to the mergers are **no longer separately determinable** from the consolidated statements.
- **xAI's standalone financial statements** are not separately provided.
- **X Holdings' (Twitter/X) standalone financial statements** are not separately provided.

Under **purchase accounting**, the acquirer's pre-acquisition financials would remain intact, and the acquiree's pre-acquisition financials would typically be disclosed separately in business combination disclosures. An investor could compare the standalone acquirer's historical performance to the post-combination performance.

---

### 2. Fair Value of Acquired Assets and Liabilities (No Step-Up in Basis)

The filing states: *"The Company's consolidated financial statements have been prepared to reflect the retrospective combination of the net assets of the entities at their historical carrying amounts for all periods presented."*

Under common-control accounting, all assets and liabilities of xAI and X Holdings are carried at their **pre-existing historical book values** — the same values recorded on the predecessor entities' books prior to the mergers.

Under **purchase accounting (ASC 805)**, all acquired assets and assumed liabilities would be measured at **acquisition-date fair value** (a "step-up" in basis). This would reveal:
- The fair value of X/Twitter's platform, user base, content, data center infrastructure, and other tangible assets
- The fair value of xAI's technology, Grok AI models, compute infrastructure, and intellectual property
- Fair value adjustments to debt, lease obligations, and other liabilities

**None of these fair values are observable** in the filing because no purchase price allocation was performed.

---

### 3. Goodwill and Identifiable Intangible Assets

The filing states explicitly: *"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded."*

Under purchase accounting, the difference between the consideration transferred and the net fair value of acquired assets and liabilities would be recorded as **goodwill**, and separately identifiable intangible assets (customer relationships, technology, brand/trademarks, in-process R&D) would be recognized and amortized over their useful lives.

Under common-control accounting:
- **No goodwill is recognized** from the mergers
- **No new intangible assets** are recorded (e.g., no customer relationship assets from X/Twitter's ~600M+ user base, no technology intangibles from Grok AI)
- There is **no observable indication of the implied premium** Musk paid (or the implied enterprise value) for these combined entities

This is particularly significant given that X Holdings was acquired by Musk in October 2022 (reportedly at ~$44 billion), and xAI raised over $31.7 billion in preferred capital across Series A through E rounds. Under purchase accounting, the fair value of consideration transferred and resulting goodwill would have been disclosed.

---

### 4. Purchase Price Allocation and Acquisition-Related Disclosures

Under purchase accounting, ASC 805 requires detailed disclosures including:
- The fair value of consideration transferred
- Contingent consideration arrangements
- Acquisition-related costs
- Revenue and earnings of the acquiree since the acquisition date
- Pro forma combined results as if the acquisition had occurred at the beginning of the period

**None of these disclosures are provided** in the filing. The common-control approach requires none of them, rendering this information unobservable.

---

### 5. Intercompany Transaction Economics

The filing states: *"All historical related party transactions between the entities have been eliminated in consolidation."*

Under common-control accounting, intercompany transactions are eliminated **for all historical periods** (retroactively). This means the standalone economics of relationships such as SpaceX providing launch services to xAI, or xAI providing AI capabilities to the X platform, are **invisible** in all periods presented.

Under purchase accounting, intercompany elimination would begin only from the **acquisition date forward**, and pre-acquisition related-party transactions would still be visible in the acquirer's historical standalone statements.

---

### 6. Individual Entity Capital Structures and Equity History

The filing explains that capital stock and shareholders' equity is presented as *"a continuation of the historical SpaceX capital stock and shareholders' equity, combined with the historical capital stock and shareholders' equity of X and xAI merged under common control, as adjusted by the respective exchange ratios."*

The exchange ratios (2.776× for X → xAI; 0.1433× for xAI → SpaceX, pre-stock-split) are applied retroactively. This means:
- **Individual entity equity structures** (authorized shares, classes, preferences) are blended
- **Historical share counts** reflect the combined, as-converted view rather than the actual shares outstanding of each standalone entity
- The only exception is **xAI's redeemable convertible preferred stock**, which is presented as its own class until the merger date (not retrospectively recast)

Under purchase accounting, the acquirer's historical equity structure would remain intact, and only the post-acquisition equity structure would reflect the combination.

---

### Summary Table

| Information Category | Observable Under Purchase Accounting | Observable Under Common-Control (as filed) |
|---|---|---|
| Standalone financials of SpaceX | ✅ Pre-acquisition periods preserved | ❌ Blended with xAI and X for all periods |
| Standalone financials of acquirees (xAI, X) | ✅ Typically disclosed separately | ❌ Not provided |
| Fair value of acquired assets/liabilities | ✅ Measured at acquisition-date fair value | ❌ Carried at historical book value |
| Goodwill from mergers | ✅ Recognized as residual | ❌ Not recorded |
| Identifiable intangible assets | ✅ Separately recognized and amortized | ❌ Not recorded |
| Purchase price allocation details | ✅ Required under ASC 805 | ❌ Not applicable |
| Pre-acquisition intercompany transactions | ✅ Visible in standalone acquirer statements | ❌ Eliminated retroactively for all periods |
| Individual entity capital structures | ✅ Acquirer's historical equity preserved | ❌ Blended retroactively |

**Bottom line:** The common-control accounting treatment means that investors cannot observe the **standalone financial profile of SpaceX prior to absorbing xAI and X**, the **fair value of the combined enterprise's assets** (particularly the X/Twitter platform and xAI's AI technology), the **implied goodwill or premium** inherent in the transactions, or the **individual operating economics** of each predecessor entity. All historical data is a blended, retrospective consolidation at historical book values — a fundamentally different informational lens than purchase accounting would provide.
